All of God’s people are praying people. Here it is taught that earnest persistence in prayer for spiritual blessings is important. The widow's earnestness prevailed even with an unjust judge; she might have feared that it would make him more against her, but our earnest prayers are pleasing to our God. Even until the end, there will still be reason for the same complaint about a lack of faith.

This parable was meant to convince some who trusted in themselves that they were righteous and looked down on others. God sees with what attitude and purpose we come to him in holy services. What the Pharisee said shows that he trusted in himself as being righteous. We can suppose that he was free from major, scandalous sins. All of this was very well and commendable. Miserable is the condition of those who fall short of the righteousness of this Pharisee, yet he was not accepted; and why? He went up to the temple to pray, but he was full of himself and his own goodness; he did not think it worthwhile to ask for God’s favor and grace. Let us beware of presenting proud prayers to the Lord, and of looking down on others. The tax collector's prayer to God was full of humility, repentance for sin, and a desire toward God. His prayer was short but to the point: 'God, be merciful to me, a sinner.' Blessed be God that we have this short prayer recorded as an answered prayer; and that we are sure that he who prayed it went to his house justified; for so we will be if we pray it, as he did, through Jesus Christ. He acknowledged himself a sinner by nature, by practice, and guilty before God. He had no dependence but on the mercy of God; he relied only on that. And God’s glory is to resist the proud and give grace to the humble. Justification comes from God in Christ; therefore those who condemn themselves are justified before God, not those who think they are righteous.

No one is too young or too small to be brought to Christ, who knows how to show kindness to those unable to do service for him. It is Christ’s desire that little children should be brought to him. The promise is to us and to our descendants; therefore he will welcome them with us. And we must receive his kingdom as children, not by purchase, but as a gift from our Father.

Many have many commendable qualities, yet they perish for lack of one thing; so this ruler could not accept Christ's terms, which would separate him from his possessions. Many are reluctant to leave Christ, yet they do leave him. After a long struggle between their convictions and their corruptions, their corruptions win out. They are very sorry that they cannot serve both, but if one must be abandoned, it will be their God, not their worldly gain. Their boasted obedience will be found to be mere outward show; the love of the world in some form lies at the root. People tend to talk too much about what they have left and lost, what they have done and suffered for Christ, as Peter did. But we should rather be ashamed that there has been any regret or difficulty in doing it.

The Spirit of Christ, in the Old Testament prophets, testified beforehand about his sufferings and the glory that would follow. The disciples’ prejudices were so strong that they could not understand these things literally. They were so focused on the prophecies that spoke of Christ's glory that they overlooked those that spoke of his suffering. People make mistakes because they read their Bibles selectively, only wanting to hear pleasant things. We are as slow to learn the proper lessons from the sufferings, crucifixion, and resurrection of Christ as the disciples were when he told them about those events; and for the same reason: self-love and a desire for worldly possessions cloud our understanding.

This poor blind man sat by the roadside begging. He was not only blind but also poor, a fitting symbol of the world of mankind that Christ came to heal and save. The prayer of faith, guided by Christ’s encouraging promises and grounded in them, will not be in vain. The grace of Christ ought to be thankfully acknowledged, to the glory of God. It is for the glory of God if we follow Jesus, as those whose eyes are opened will do. We must praise God for his mercies to others as well as for our own. If we want to understand these things rightly, we must come to Christ like the blind man, earnestly asking him to open our eyes and show us clearly the excellence of his commands and the value of his salvation.
